misconceptions.name: Dimension-blind product
misconceptions.description: Multiplies matrices entrywise or ignores shape compatibility.

misconceptions.name: Commutativity assumption
misconceptions.description: Assumes A B = B A in general.

misconceptions.name: Always-unique belief
misconceptions.description: Assumes every linear system has exactly one solution.

misconceptions.name: Inverse-always-exists belief
misconceptions.description: Inverts a matrix without checking invertibility, or writes 1/A entrywise.

misconceptions.name: Determinant linearity error
misconceptions.description: Believes det(A + B) = det A + det B or det(cA) = c det A for n > 1.

misconceptions.name: Independence by count
misconceptions.description: Judges independence by how many vectors there are, not by combinations.

misconceptions.name: Rank equals row count
misconceptions.description: Confuses the number of rows or equations with the rank.

misconceptions.name: Zero-vector eigenvector
misconceptions.description: Offers the zero vector as an eigenvector, or rejects eigenvalue 0 as impossible.

misconceptions.name: Eigenvalue-of-sum error
misconceptions.description: Assumes eigenvalues of A + B are sums of eigenvalues of A and B.

misconceptions.name: Projection idempotence miss
misconceptions.description: Expects projecting twice to move the vector again; misses P^2 = P.

misconceptions.name: Normal-equations blindness
misconceptions.description: Solves A x = b directly when the system is inconsistent instead of least squares.

misconceptions.name: Orthogonal-means-perpendicular-entries
misconceptions.description: Thinks a matrix is orthogonal when entries look symmetric, not when Q^T Q = I.

items.stem: A is 2x3 and B is 3x2. Which product is defined, and what is its shape?

choices.text: AB is 2x2 and BA is 3x3; both are defined
Only AB is defined; BA is not
AB = BA whenever both are defined
Neither is defined because the shapes differ

items.stem: A is 3x3 with det A = 2. What is det(2A)?
Which statement about eigenvectors is true?

choices.text: An eigenvector must be nonzero, but eigenvalue 0 is allowed
The zero vector is an eigenvector for every eigenvalue
A matrix with eigenvalue 0 cannot exist
Eigenvalues of A + B are the sums of eigenvalues of A and B

items.stem: Three vectors in R^3. Which conclusion is valid?

choices.text: They may be independent or dependent; test the only-trivial-combination condition
Three vectors in R^3 are always independent
Three vectors always span R^3
Independence is impossible to determine without lengths

items.stem: P projects onto a subspace. What is P(P(v))?

choices.text: P(v): projecting an already-projected vector changes nothing
A vector strictly closer to the subspace than P(v)
The zero vector
v itself

nodes.description: Spirals from complex pairs; generalized eigenvectors.

nodes.title: Matrix exponential
nodes.description: e^{At} as the flow of the system; computing it via diagonalization.

nodes.description: Indicial equation; why Bessel-type equations need Frobenius.

misconceptions.name: Lost constant of integration
misconceptions.description: Drops the arbitrary constant, or adds it before integrating both sides.

misconceptions.name: Particular-as-general
misconceptions.description: Presents one particular solution as the general solution.

misconceptions.name: Division by a vanishing factor
misconceptions.description: Divides by an expression that can be zero and silently loses solutions.

misconceptions.name: Integrating factor misapplied
misconceptions.description: Multiplies by e^{int p} but forgets the left side must become (mu y)'.

misconceptions.name: Characteristic-equation sign error
misconceptions.description: Builds the characteristic polynomial with flipped signs from the ODE.

misconceptions.name: Complex roots discarded
misconceptions.description: Treats complex characteristic roots as no solution instead of oscillation.

misconceptions.name: Resonant guess collision
misconceptions.description: Uses an undetermined-coefficients guess that duplicates a homogeneous solution.

misconceptions.name: Laplace derivative rule dropped ICs
misconceptions.description: Uses L{y'} = s Y instead of s Y - y(0).

misconceptions.name: Eigenvalue-free system solving
misconceptions.description: Tries to solve x' = A x componentwise, ignoring the coupling.

misconceptions.name: Equilibrium stability by sign of value
misconceptions.description: Judges stability by the sign of the equilibrium value, not the derivative test.

misconceptions.name: Interval-of-validity blindness
misconceptions.description: Extends a solution across a singularity of the equation.

misconceptions.name: Wronskian as a number for all x
misconceptions.description: Evaluates the Wronskian at one point incorrectly generalized, or treats a zero value at a point as dependence everywhere.

items.stem: y'' + 4y = 0. Which is the general solution?

choices.text: C1 cos(2x) + C2 sin(2x)
No real solution exists because the roots are complex
cos(2x) + sin(2x)

items.stem: Taking the Laplace transform of y' with y(0) = 3 gives:

choices.text: s Y(s) - 3
s Y(s)
Y(s) / s
s Y(s) + 3

items.stem: y'' - y = e^x. What particular-solution guess works?

choices.text: A x e^x, because e^x already solves the homogeneous equation
A e^x
A constant
A cos x + B sin x

items.stem: Solving dy/dx = y^2 by separation, dividing by y^2 requires what caution?

choices.text: y = 0 is a solution that division silently discards; note it separately
No caution; division is always valid
The constant of integration is unnecessary here
Separation does not apply to this equation

nodes.description: Fourier optics, diffusion in semiconductors, waveguides: the applied spine.

misconceptions.name: Coefficient formula mix-up
misconceptions.description: Swaps the an and bn integrals, or forgets the 1/L normalization.

misconceptions.name: Parity blindness
misconceptions.description: Computes all coefficients for an odd or even function instead of using symmetry.

misconceptions.name: Convergence-at-jump error
misconceptions.description: Believes the series converges to a one-sided value at a jump instead of the midpoint.

misconceptions.name: Gibbs disappears with more terms
misconceptions.description: Expects the Gibbs overshoot to shrink to zero as terms are added.

misconceptions.name: Separation without homogeneous BCs
misconceptions.description: Applies separation of variables directly despite nonhomogeneous boundary data.

misconceptions.name: Sign error in the separated ODEs
misconceptions.description: Chooses the wrong sign for the separation constant and gets growing modes on a rod.

misconceptions.name: Heat and wave conflation
misconceptions.description: Uses oscillatory time behavior for the heat equation or decaying for the wave equation.

misconceptions.name: d'Alembert direction error
misconceptions.description: Writes f(x + ct) as the right-moving wave.

misconceptions.name: Laplace as evolution
misconceptions.description: Treats Laplace's equation as if it has an initial condition to march in time.

misconceptions.name: Orthogonality assumed for any two functions
misconceptions.description: Assumes any two eigenfunction-looking functions are orthogonal without the S-L structure.

items.stem: A Fourier series at a jump discontinuity of f converges to:

choices.text: The average of the left and right limits
The left-hand value
The right-hand value
It diverges at the jump

items.stem: u_t = alpha u_xx and u_tt = c^2 u_xx differ how in time behavior of each spatial mode?

choices.text: Heat modes decay exponentially; wave modes oscillate
Both oscillate
Both decay
Heat modes oscillate; wave modes decay

items.stem: In u(x, t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct), the term f(x - ct) represents:

choices.text: A shape moving right with speed c
A shape moving left with speed c
A standing wave
A decaying mode

items.stem: f(x) = x on [-pi, pi] is odd. Its Fourier series therefore:

choices.text: Has only sine terms; a0 and all an vanish by symmetry
Has only cosine terms
Requires computing all coefficients; symmetry gives no shortcut
Has a nonzero a0 because f is nonzero

misconceptions.name: Limit equals plug-in always
misconceptions.description: Evaluates every limit by substitution, treating all functions as continuous.

misconceptions.name: 0/0 means no limit
misconceptions.description: Concludes a limit does not exist whenever direct substitution gives 0/0.

misconceptions.name: Product rule denial
misconceptions.description: Differentiates a product as the product of derivatives.

misconceptions.name: Dropped inner derivative
misconceptions.description: Applies the chain rule without multiplying by the inner derivative.

misconceptions.name: Continuous implies differentiable
misconceptions.description: Assumes continuity guarantees a derivative; forgets corners and cusps.

misconceptions.name: Critical point equals extremum
misconceptions.description: Declares every critical point a max or min without a test.

misconceptions.name: Lost constant of integration
misconceptions.description: Omits + C from indefinite integrals.

misconceptions.name: FTC lower-limit blindness
misconceptions.description: Evaluates F(b) only, or differentiates an accumulation function incorrectly.

items.stem: lim as x -> 2 of (x^2 - 4)/(x - 2). Direct substitution gives 0/0. Therefore:

choices.text: Factor and cancel: the limit is 4
The limit does not exist because of the 0/0
The limit is 0
The limit is undefined, same as f(2)

items.stem: d/dx of sin(x^2) is:

choices.text: 2x cos(x^2)
cos(x^2)
2x cos(2x)
-2x cos(x^2)

nodes.description: Radicals of quadratics via sin, tan, sec substitutions.

misconceptions.name: Parts sign slip
misconceptions.description: Writes integration by parts as uv + int v du, or misassigns u and dv.

misconceptions.name: Substitution without the bounds
misconceptions.description: Changes variables in a definite integral but keeps the old limits.

misconceptions.name: Improper treated as proper
misconceptions.description: Evaluates across an infinite limit or singularity without a limit process.

misconceptions.name: nth-term test proves convergence
misconceptions.description: Concludes a series converges because its terms go to zero.

misconceptions.name: Ratio test equals one is conclusive
misconceptions.description: Reads L = 1 in the ratio test as convergence or divergence.

misconceptions.name: Unchecked endpoints
misconceptions.description: States an interval of convergence without testing the endpoints.

misconceptions.name: Infinite series as big finite sum
misconceptions.description: Manipulates divergent or conditionally convergent series as if finite.

misconceptions.name: Remainder ignored
misconceptions.description: Uses a Taylor polynomial with no sense of where or how much it errs.

items.stem: The terms of sum 1/n go to zero. The series therefore:

choices.text: May still diverge, and this one does (harmonic series)
Converges, because the terms go to zero
Converges to 1
Converges by the ratio test with L = 1

items.stem: Evaluating int from 0 to 2 of 2x (x^2 + 1)^3 dx with u = x^2 + 1, the new bounds are:

choices.text: u from 1 to 5
u from 0 to 2, unchanged
u from 0 to 5
Bounds are irrelevant for substitution

misconceptions.name: Partial forgets to hold constant
misconceptions.description: Differentiates all variables at once when taking a partial.

misconceptions.name: Scalar gradient
misconceptions.description: Treats the gradient as a number instead of a vector of partials.

misconceptions.name: Unnormalized direction
misconceptions.description: Computes directional derivatives with a non-unit direction vector.

misconceptions.name: 1D test in 2D
misconceptions.description: Classifies critical points by f_xx alone, ignoring the mixed partial.

misconceptions.name: Bounds order confusion
misconceptions.description: Sets iterated-integral bounds that do not describe the region.

misconceptions.name: Dropped Jacobian
misconceptions.description: Converts to polar or spherical without the r or rho^2 sin(phi) factor.

misconceptions.name: Orientation blindness
misconceptions.description: Ignores curve or surface orientation in line and flux integrals.

misconceptions.name: Theorem without hypotheses
misconceptions.description: Applies Green or Stokes on non-closed curves or with singularities inside.

items.stem: Converting a double integral to polar coordinates, dA becomes:

choices.text: r dr d(theta)
dr d(theta)
r^2 dr d(theta)
dx dy, unchanged

items.stem: At a critical point, f_xx > 0 and f_yy > 0. Can you conclude a local minimum?

choices.text: Not yet: the discriminant f_xx f_yy - f_xy^2 must be positive too
Yes: both second partials positive means minimum
Yes: any positive second partial suffices
No: it must be a saddle

nodes.description: Inverting conditionals; base rates and posterior probability.

misconceptions.name: Confusion of the inverse
misconceptions.description: Equates P(A given B) with P(B given A); neglects base rates.

misconceptions.name: Independence by default
misconceptions.description: Multiplies probabilities without checking independence.

misconceptions.name: Gambler's fallacy
misconceptions.description: Believes past independent outcomes change future probabilities.

misconceptions.name: Unnormalized probabilities
misconceptions.description: Assigns probabilities that do not sum or integrate to one.

misconceptions.name: Variance shortcut misuse
misconceptions.description: Uses (E[X])^2 for E[X^2], or Var(X + Y) = Var X + Var Y without independence.

misconceptions.name: SD-SE conflation
misconceptions.description: Uses the sample standard deviation where the standard error belongs.

misconceptions.name: CI as probability of the parameter
misconceptions.description: Reads a 95 percent CI as a 95 percent chance the parameter is inside.

misconceptions.name: p-value as truth probability
misconceptions.description: Reads the p-value as the probability the null hypothesis is true.

misconceptions.name: Correlation implies causation
misconceptions.description: Infers causal influence from association alone.

misconceptions.name: Normality everywhere
misconceptions.description: Invokes the CLT for tiny samples or heavy-tailed data.

items.stem: A test is 99 percent accurate; the condition affects 1 in 1000. A person tests positive. The chance they have the condition is:

choices.text: Well under 50 percent: the false positives outnumber the true ones
99 percent, the accuracy of the test
About 1 in 1000, unchanged
Exactly 50 percent

items.stem: A study reports p = 0.03. This means:

choices.text: Data this extreme occurs 3 percent of the time if the null is true
There is a 3 percent chance the null hypothesis is true
There is a 97 percent chance the alternative is true
The effect size is large

nodes.title: Functions and relations
nodes.description: Injective, surjective, bijective; equivalence relations.

nodes.title: Sum and product rules
nodes.description: Decomposing counting problems into stages and cases.

nodes.title: Permutations and combinations
nodes.description: Ordered vs unordered selection; binomial coefficients.

nodes.title: Pigeonhole and inclusion-exclusion
nodes.description: Guaranteed collisions; counting unions without double counting.

nodes.title: Recurrence relations
nodes.description: Modeling with recurrences; solving linear cases in closed form.

nodes.title: Modular arithmetic
nodes.description: Congruences, inverses mod m, and applications.

nodes.title: Graphs: basics and degree
nodes.description: Vertices, edges, degree, handshake lemma.

nodes.title: Connectivity, paths, and trees
nodes.description: Euler paths, tree properties, spanning trees in brief.

nodes.title: Growth of functions and Big-O
nodes.description: Comparing algorithm growth; dominant terms.

misconceptions.name: Converse assumed
misconceptions.description: Reads p implies q as also meaning q implies p.

misconceptions.name: Quantifier negation error
misconceptions.description: Negates for-all as for-all-not instead of there-exists-not.

misconceptions.name: Induction without a base
misconceptions.description: Runs the inductive step with no base case, or the wrong one.

misconceptions.name: Order confusion in counting
misconceptions.description: Uses permutations where order is irrelevant, or combinations where it matters.

misconceptions.name: Double counting
misconceptions.description: Counts overlapping cases twice; skips inclusion-exclusion.

misconceptions.name: Ordinary division mod m
misconceptions.description: Divides both sides of a congruence without a modular inverse.

misconceptions.name: Proof by example
misconceptions.description: Verifies a universal claim on a few cases and declares it proved.

misconceptions.name: Big-O reads constants
misconceptions.description: Ranks growth by constants and lower-order terms instead of dominant term.

items.stem: The negation of 'every student passed' is:

choices.text: At least one student did not pass
Every student did not pass
No student passed
Most students did not pass

items.stem: Choosing a 3-person committee from 10 people:

choices.text: C(10, 3) = 120: order does not matter
P(10, 3) = 720: order matters
